html:
III

Zeddy

вАЬGreat balls of fire! Looka here! See mah luck!вАЭ Jake stopped in his tracksвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ went onвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ stopped againвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ retraced his stepsвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ checked himself. вАЬGuess I wonвАЩt go back right now. Never let a woman think youвАЩre too crazy about her. But sheвАЩs a particularly sweet piece a business.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Me and her again tonight.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Handful oвАЩ luck shot straight outa heaven. Oh, boy! Harlem is mine!вАЭ

Jake went rolling along Fifth Avenue. He crossed over to Lenox Avenue and went into Uncle DocвАЩs saloon, where he had left his bag. Called for a glass of Scotch. вАЬGimme the siphon, Doc. IвАЩm off the straight stuff.вАЭ

вАЬIszh you? Counta what?вАЭ

вАЬHits the belly better this way. I lвАЩarned it over the other side.вАЭ

A slap on the shoulder brought him sharply round. вАЬZeddy Plummer! What grave is you arisen from?вАЭ he cried.

вАЬBuddy, you looks so good to me, I could kish you,вАЭ Zeddy said.

вАЬWhere?вАЭ

вАЬEverywhere.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ French style.вАЭ

вАЬOne on one cheek and one on the other.вАЭ

вАЬSavee-vous?вАЭ

вАЬParlee-vous?вАЭ

Uncle Doc set another glass on the counter and poured out pure Bourbon. Zeddy reached a little above JakeвАЩs shoulders. He was stocky, thick-shouldered, flat-footed, and walked like a bear. Some more customers came in and the buddies eased round to the short side of the bar.

вАЬWhat part of the earth done belch you out?вАЭ demanded Zeddy. вАЬNevah heared no GodвАЩs tidings a you sence we missed you from Brest.вАЭ

вАЬAnd how about you?вАЭ Jake countered. вАЬDidnвАЩt them Germans git you scrambling over the top?вАЭ

вАЬNevah seeвАЩd them, buddy. None a them showed the goose-step around Brest. Have a shot on me.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Well, dawg bite me, butвБ†вАФsay, Jake, weвАЩve got some more stuff to booze over.вАЭ

Zeddy slapped Jake on his breast and looked him over again. вАЬThaвАЩs some stuff youвАЩre strutting in, boh. вАЩTainвАЩt вАЩMerican and it ainвАЩt French.вАЭвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬEnglish.вАЭ Jake showed his clean white teeth.

вАЬMah granny anвАЩ me! You been in that theah white folksвАЩ country, too?вАЭ

вАЬAnd donвАЩt I look as if Ise been? Where else could a fellow git such good and cheap man clothes to cover his skin?вАЭ

вАЬBuddy, I know itвАЩs the trute. What you doing today?вАЭ

вАЬNo, when you make me think ovit, particular thing. And you?вАЭ

вАЬIвАЩm alongshore butвБ†вАФI ainвАЩt agwine to work thisaday.вАЭ

вАЬI guess IвАЩve got to be heaving along right back to it, too, in pretty short time. I got to get me a room butвБ†вАФвАЭ

Uncle Doc reminded Jake that his suitcase was there.

вАЬI ainвАЩt nevah fohgitting all mah worldly goods,вАЭ responded Jake.

Zeddy took Jake to a poolroom where they played. Jake was the better man. From the poolroom they went to Aunt HattieвАЩs chitterling joint in 132nd Street, where they fed. Fricassee chicken and rice. Green peas. Stewed corn.

Aunt HattieвАЩs was renowned among the lowly of HarlemвАЩs Black Belt. It was a little basement joint, smoke-colored. And Aunt Hattie was weather-beaten dark-brown, cheery-faced, with two rusty-red front teeth sticking together conspicuously out of her twisted, spread-away mouth. She cooked delicious foodвБ†вАФhome-cooked food they called it. None of the boys loafing round that section of Fifth Avenue would dream of going to any other place for their вАЬpoke chops.вАЭ

Aunt Hattie admired her new customer from the kitchen door and he quite filled her sight. And when she went with the dish rag to wipe the oilcloth before setting down the coconut pie, she rubbed her breast against JakeвАЩs shoulder and a sensual light gleamed in her aged smoke-red eyes.

The buddies talked about the days of Brest. Zeddy recalled the everlasting unloading and unloading of ships and the toting of lumber. The house of the Young MenвАЩs Christian Association, overlooking the harbor, where colored soldiers were not wanted.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The central Rue de Siam and the point near the Prefecture of Marine, from which you could look down on the red lights of the Quartier R√©serv√©. The fatal fights between black men and white in the maisons closes. The encounters between apaches and white Americans. The French sailors that couldnвАЩt get the Yankee idea of amour and men. And the cemetery, just beyond the old medieval gate of the town, where he left his second-best buddy.

вАЬPoor boh. Was always bellyaching for a chance over the top. Nevah got it nor nothing. Not even a baid in the hospital. Strong like a bull, yet just knocked off in the dark through raw cracker cussedness.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Some life it was, buddy, in them days. We was always on the defensive as if the boches, as the froggies called them, was right down on us.вАЭ

вАЬYet you stuck tвАЩrough it toting lumber. Got back to Harlem all right, though.вАЭ

вАЬYou bet I did, boh. You kain trust Zeddy Plummer to look out for his own black hide.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But you, buddy. How come you just vanished thataway like a spook? How did you take your tail out ovit?вАЭ

Jake told Zeddy how he walked out of it straight to the station in Brest. Le Havre. London. The West India Docks. And back home to Harlem.

вАЬBut you must keep it dark, buddy,вАЭ Zeddy cautioned. вАЬDonвАЩt go shooting off your mouth too free. GovвАЩmant still smoking out deserters and draft dodgers.вАЭ

вАЬI ainвАЩt told no nigger but you, boh. Nor ofay, neither. Ahm in your confidence, chappie.вАЭ

вАЬThatвАЩs all right, buddy.вАЭ Zeddy put his hand on JakeвАЩs knee. вАЬItвАЩs better to keep your business close all the time. But IвАЩll tell you this for your perticular information. Niggers am awful closemouthed in some things. There is fellows here in Harlem that just telled the draft to mount upstairs. Pohlice and soldiers were hunting evвАЩwhere foh them. And they was right here in Harlem. Fifty dollars apiece foh them. All their friends knowed it and not a one gived them in. I tell you, niggers am amazing sometimes. Yet other times, without any natural reason, they will just go vomiting out their guts to the ofays about one another.вАЭ

вАЬGod; but itвАЩs good to get back home again!вАЭ said Jake.

вАЬI should think you was hungry foh a liвАЩl brown honey. I tell you trute, buddy. I made mine ovah there, spitinвАЩ ov evвАЩthing. I lвАЩarned her a little zвАЩinglise and she lвАЩarned me beaucoup plus the French stuff.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ The real stuff, buddy. But I was tearinвАЩ mad and glad to get back all the same. Take it from me, buddy, there ainвАЩt no honey lak to that theah comes out of our own belonging-to-us honeycomb.вАЭ

вАЬMan, what you telling me?вАЭ cried Jake. вАЬDonвАЩt I knows it? What else you think made me leave over the other side? And dog mah doggone ef I didnвАЩt find it just as I landed.вАЭ

вАЬKвАСhhhhhhh! KвАСhhhhhhhh!вАЭ Zeddy laughed. вАЬDog mah cats! You done tasted the real life aвАЩready?вАЭ

вАЬLast night was the end of the world, buddy, and tonight ahm going back there,вАЭ chanted Jake as he rose and began kicking up his heels round the joint.

Zeddy also got up and put on his gray cap. They went back to the poolroom. Jake met two more fellows that he knew and got into a ring of ZeddyвАЩs pals.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Most of them were longshoremen. There was plenty of work, Jake learned. Before he left the poolroom he and Zeddy agreed to meet the next evening at Uncle DocвАЩs.

вАЬGot to work tomorrow, boh,вАЭ Zeddy informed Jake.

вАЬGood old New York! The same old wench of a city. Elevated racketing over youвАЩ head. Subway bellowing under youвАЩ feet. Me foh wrastling round them piers again. Scratching down to the bottom of them ships and scrambling out. All alongshore for me now. No more fooling with the sea. Same old New York. Everybody dashing round like crazy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Same old New York. But the ofay faces am different from those ovah across the pond. Sure they is. Stiffer. Tighter. Yes, they is that.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ But the sun does better here than over there. And the skyвАЩs so high and dry and blue. And the air itвБ†вАФO Gawd it works in youвАЩ flesh and blood like Scotch. O Lawdy, Lawdy! I wants to live to a hundred and finish mah days in New York.вАЭ

Jake threw himself up as if to catch the air pouring down from the blue sky.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬHarlem! Harlem! Little thicker, little darker and noisier and smellier, but Harlem just the same. The niggers done plowed through 130th Street. Heading straight foh 125th. Spades beyond Eighth Avenue. Going, going, going Harlem! Going up! Nevah befoh I seed so many dickty shines in sich swell motorcars. Plenty moh nigger shops. Seventh Avenue done gone high-brown. O Lawdy! Harlem bigger, Harlem betterвБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ and sweeter.вАЭ

вАЬStreet and streets! 132nd, 33rd, 34th. It wasnвАЩt 135th and it wasnвАЩt beyond theah.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ O Lawd! how did I fohgit to remember the street and number. I reeled outa there like a drunken man. I been so happy.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶

вАЬ34th, 32nd, 33rd.вБ†вАКвБ†вА¶ Only difference in the name. All the streets am just the same and all the houses вАЩlike as peas. I could try this one heah or that one there butвБ†вАФRabbit foot! I didnвАЩt even git her name. Oh, Jakie, Jake! What a big Ah-Ah you is.

вАЬI was a fool not to go back right then when I feeled like it. What did I want to tighten up mahself and crow and strut like a crazy cat for? A grand Ah-Ah I is. Feet in mah hands! Take me back to the Baltimore tonight. I ainвАЩt gwine to know no peace till I lay these here hands on mah tantalizing brown again.вАЭ
